note: （彭博社观点）-迈克·诺沃格拉茨（Mike Novogratz）以从华尔街跳槽到加密货币而闻名，他定期将全球大流行，金融刺激与比特币作为避风港的吸引力联系起来。他的加密公司Galaxy Digital Holdings Ltd.在八月份的报纸广告中大声说道：“现在是投资比特币的时候了。”当时，诺沃格拉茨说：“如果我认为我们要治好Covid，突然之间每个人都将在财政上保持保守，我会更加担心[价格下跌]。”比特币现在的交易价格接近18,000美元，在六个月内上涨了近100％，并在2017年创下历史新高（考虑到紧随其后的丑陋崩溃，忠实的比特币人宁愿忘记）。 Galaxy的股票表现更好，上涨了380％。加密货币中最大的信托提供商-灰度比特币信托公司（Grayscale Bitcoin Trust）的总资产今年增长了四倍，达到84亿美元，但这是否真的是受到人们寻求免受更加不确定世界的保护的推动呢？ Covid-19疫苗越来越近；唐纳德·特朗普（Donald Trump）的临时顾问史蒂夫·班农（Steve Bannon）为比特币加油打气，这是“全球民粹主义叛乱”的一部分。全球股市接近历史高位。如果有的话，比特币看起来更像是类固醇的股票市场，而不是数字版本的黄金，自10月底以来，由于对Covid疗法的信心逐渐增强，数字黄金几乎没有动摇。 。你会看到为什么对冲基金怀疑论者雷·达里奥（Ray Dalio）怀疑比特币的魅力。当一切都在上涨的时候，加密货币最近与股票的高于平均水平的关联就很好了，但是在压力时期却不是这样：例如在3月中旬， Covid触发的安全性将比特币的价格降低了一半。堪萨斯城联储最近进行的一项研究比较了1995年至2020年2月的债券，黄金和比特币，发现美国国债的表现“始终如一”，是“避风港”，黄金“偶尔”是“永远”。比特币的背后是现实。不稳定且仍具有投机性的资产，可能会导致价格大幅波动。大量的投资者资金正在转移到加密货币交易所，部分原因是有风险的交易机会浮出水面：具有魅力的对冲基金转换人Paul Tudor Jones将比特币比作“尽早投资Google”。聪明的钱背后是激动的零售赌客，他们显然是用他们的1200美元大流行性刺激支票购买了比特币。虽然像PayPal Holdings Inc. 。 Chainalysis的数据估计，在2019年中期至2020年中期之间，北美地区的商家仅占加密活动的1％，而交易所则占近90％。在世界上最大的加密货币市场东亚，比特币交易是由Tether驱动的，Tether是一种美元替代品，被卷入纽约诉讼中，涉嫌掩盖损失。财富的变化，而不是破坏普通人的货币使用方式。达利奥感到困惑，达利奥本周想知道他在2017年将比特币称为泡沫后是否``错过了某些东西''。从根本上说，没有什么能解决比特币作为货币或商店的弱点他说，它的价值不容小if，如果它成为对政府和中央银行的威胁，它将受到监管者的重视。他指出：比特币看起来仍然像是一种具有泡沫般价格动态的资产，其2100万供应上限的人为稀缺以及其助手中的``HODLing''心态夸大了这一事实，据估计，这些硬币中约有60％是优秀的硬币。当然，您可以说很多事情都是泡沫，包括由特斯拉（Tesla Inc.）和空白支票的SPAC推动的股市。但是，当JKRowling和Maisie Williams等名人在Twitter上公开要求加密提示时，就证明了比特币炒作的独特力量。像Novogratz和Elon Musk这样的人乐意提供看涨的建议。鉴于这种繁荣，2020年的价格飙升是否意味着2021年的大跌？不必要。但是，推动比特币价格上涨的力量并不像其捍卫者所坚持的那样简单。 Novogratz认为今年是一个里程碑：“我们已经超越了Rubicon……比特币现在已成为一种资产，”他在八月表示。但是要使大型银行和消费者真正相信它是一个稳定的银行，还需要花费第二个步骤才能达到20,000美元。本专栏不一定反映编辑委员会或彭博社及其所有者的观点.Lionel Laurent是彭博社观点专栏作家涵盖了欧洲联盟和法国。他曾在路透社和福布斯工作，如需更多文章，请访问Bloomberg.com/opinion立即访问我们，以获取最受信任的商业新闻来源的最新信息。©2020 Bloomberg L.P.
note_en: (Bloomberg Opinion) -- Mike Novogratz, famous for making the jump from Wall Street to cryptocurrencies, has regularly drawn a link between the global pandemic, financial stimulus and the allure of Bitcoin as a safe haven. “Now is the time to invest in Bitcoin,” his crypto firm Galaxy Digital Holdings Ltd. thundered in a newspaper ad in August. “I’d be much more nervous [about a price drop] if I thought we’re going to cure Covid and all of a sudden everyone’s going to be fiscally conservative,” Novogratz said at the time.He got one thing right. Bitcoin is now trading near $18,000, up almost 100% in six months and it’s flirting with an all-time high reached in 2017 (which, given it was followed by an ugly crash, faithful Bitcoiners would rather forget). Shares of Galaxy have done even better, rising by 380%. Total assets at Grayscale Bitcoin Trust, the biggest trust provider in the cryptocurrency, have risen fourfold this year to $8.4 billion.But is this really being driven by people seeking protection from a more uncertain world? A Covid-19 vaccine is getting closer; Donald Trump, whose one-time adviser Steve Bannon cheered Bitcoin as part of a “global populist revolt,” is on his way out. Global equity markets are near all-time highs.If anything, Bitcoin looks much more like the stock market on steroids than it does a digital version of gold, which has barely budged since the end of October as confidence about a Covid cure has gradually improved. You can see why hedge fund skeptics like Ray Dalio are dubious of Bitcoin’s charms.The cryptocurrency’s recent above-average correlation with equities is fine when everything’s going up, but not in times of stress: In mid-March, for example, a flight to safety triggered by Covid cut Bitcoin’s price in half. A recent Kansas City Fed study comparing bonds, gold and Bitcoin between 1995 and Feb. 2020 found Treasuries behaved “consistently” as a safe haven, gold “occasionally” and Bitcoin “never.”Behind the talk of digital gold is the reality of an erratic, still-speculative asset with the potential for big price swings. Serious investor money is changing hands on crypto exchanges partly because a risky trading opportunity is in the air: Charismatic hedge fund convert Paul Tudor Jones compared Bitcoin to “investing in Google early.” Right behind the smart money are the excited retail punters who apparently used their $1,200 pandemic stimulus checks to buy Bitcoin.While digital payment firms such as PayPal Holdings Inc. and Square Inc. have launched Bitcoin applications, this price jump is not about people buying cappuccinos. Data from Chainalysis estimates merchants made up only about 1% of crypto activity in North America between mid-2019 and mid-2020, while exchanges accounted for almost 90%. In East Asia, the world’s biggest crypto market, Bitcoin trading is driven by Tether, a token used as a U.S. dollar stand-in that’s embroiled in a New York lawsuit over alleged covered-up losses.Crypto is still a heady bet on life-changing wealth, not a disruptor of how normal people use money.Hence the confusion of Dalio, who wondered this week if he was “missing something” after dubbing Bitcoin a bubble back in 2017. Nothing had fundamentally fixed Bitcoin’s weakness as a currency or store of value, he says, and if it ever became a threat to governments and central banks it would be keelhauled by regulators. He has a point: Bitcoin still looks like an asset with bubble-like price dynamics, exaggerated by the artificial scarcity of its 21-million supply cap and a “HODLing” mentality among its acolytes, who hoard an estimated 60% of outstanding coins.Sure, you could call a lot of things bubbles, including stock markets supercharged by Tesla Inc. and blank-check SPACs. But the unique power of Bitcoin hype is demonstrated when celebrities such as JK Rowling and Maisie Williams ask publicly for crypto tips on Twitter. The likes of Novogratz and Elon Musk are happy to offer bullish advice.Given this exuberance, does the 2020 price spike mean a 2021 crash? Not necessarily. But the forces that drive Bitcoin’s price aren’t as straightforward as its defenders insist. Novogratz feels this year is a milestone: “We’ve crossed the Rubicon … Bitcoin is now an asset,” he said in August. But it will take more than a second trip toward $20,000 to truly convince big banks and consumers that it’s a stable one.This column does not necessarily reflect the opinion of the editorial board or Bloomberg LP and its owners.Lionel Laurent is a Bloomberg Opinion columnist covering the European Union and France. He worked previously at Reuters and Forbes.For more articles like this, please visit us at bloomberg.com/opinionSubscribe now to stay ahead with the most trusted business news source.©2020 Bloomberg L.P.

这是一个很难回答的问题，因为“伟大的”产品经理要做的事情很多。而且因为学科本身正在迅速变化。没有普遍的答案。但这是一个值得提出的问题。
  我通过采访行业领导者，利用我自己的经验和研究解决了这个问题。
  这是我收集的优秀产品经理倾向于分享的主要特征，原则和策略。
      作为产品经理，我们每天都会面临许多决定。但是“有效的决策”是什么意思？
  被“数据驱动”是一个好的开始。但是该术语被过度使用以至于失去其含义。它也有盲点。
    出色的产品经理能够在任何给定的时刻在数据和直觉之间找到适当的平衡，以告知并推动他们的决策。这可能是一个很小的错误修复，或者是一个更大的问题，例如v1中包含的功能与要剪切的内容。与我合作过的最好的产品经理能够很好地做到这一点，平衡各种输入的“数据”，并将其与他们的直觉相结合，以找到任何给定时刻在索引上的正确平衡。
      科学家和产品经理都扮演着寻求真理的游戏–科学家在自然界中检验他们的想法；产品经理在市场上对其进行测试。
Duolingo产品总监Daniel Falabella认为，“产品经理的工作90％是为了找到'真相'”。这就是丹尼尔（Daniel）认为企业家做出最佳PM的原因之一。他加了，
  许多PM都通过了这些动作：他们与队友交谈，熟悉虚荣性指标，与2位用户交谈或假设他们的经理是对的。他们给人以拥有正确输入的幻想，只是在讨论输出时才付出代价。
      产品经理负责其产品的成功。但是到达那里的途径很少是直截了当的。
    出色的PM具有适应能力。他们知道何时进入执行模式，何时退后并制定策略。他们使周围的每个人都感到被听到。归根结底，他们使客户和业务成功，他们的团队和跨职能合作伙伴希望与他们反复合作。
    Village Global和On Deck的联合创始人Erik Torenberg谈到了针对创始人的类似想法：
  创始人被低估的技能：转变高度。人们通常要么擅长高层战略，要么擅长原子级执行，但两者却很少。它具有缩小和描绘5年愿景的能力，然后深入日常杂草中，并了解如何两者连接。
      Shopify平台产品副总裁兼总经理GM Brandon Chu在一篇文章中写道，他认识的一些最佳PM都是根据第一原则做出决策的。他解释说
第一原理思考对PM有所帮助，因为随着公司规模的扩大，可以通过其团队和利益相关者可以团结起来的方式简化历史，当前和未来决策背后的基本原理交流。
  这样一来，PM周围的人们就可以朝同一个方向快速移动，脱钩并在他们不在场的情况下进行明智的权衡。
          从解决方案的想法开始并付诸实践，这是极具诱惑力的，尤其是对于技术娴熟的人们。但是要打造出优质的产品，需要了解问题并了解问题背后的人。
  三星NEXT生态系统负责人兼总经理助理Bo Ren在接受采访时解释说，
  如果您站在地面上并听到人们遭受的痛苦，那么您可以对需要做的事情有更深入的了解。不只是移情。它是具体的，并根据人们的实际经验来确定需要改进的区域。”
    Produx Labs的首席执行官兼创始人Melissa Perri在一次采访中说：“真正出色的产品经理与真正出色的产品经理不同的地方，是他们思考和解决问题的方式”。她继续说，
  对我来说，像产品经理一样思考就是解决问题。这是关于综合大量信息，了解系统，试图将问题汇总在一起，将其分解为可管理的小块以便您可以对其进行分析，然后从那里找出正确的解决方案。
丹尼尔（从早些时候开始）认为谦虚/可教练是优秀产品经理的另一个重要特征。
  与寻求真相有关，您在整个职业生涯中都会犯很多错误。对于聪明而雄心勃勃的人来说，这可能是一个艰难的选择。但这是成长为一个人并学会随着时间的推移做出更好的决定的重要组成部分。
      定义需求甚至产品交付后，您的工作就不会完成。
    如果您不厌其烦地说出来，可能您说的还不够。不断的交流听起来像是“蓬松”，但事实并非如此。传福音是这个角色的关键部分，确保组织保持一致并朝着同一方向游泳是您的工作。
    对于您[产品经理]来说，沟通是主要的“输出”，应该是特殊的。
      总是有更多的东西要建造，而没有时间。通常，您没有像您想要的那样有那么多人从事产品工作。
    这可能意味着找到更便宜的技术解决方法。这可能意味着无情地专注于真正能够移动针头的少量事物。
Dark的共同创始人，Lola的前产品副总裁Ellen Chisa在一次采访中说：“您必须真正热爱制造东西，但是您必须不要成为真正制造东西的人。
  热爱做事非常有力。这种内在动力可以使您继续前进，尤其是在遇到困难时。
  这种真正的兴趣也可以转化为您注意到生活中的其他炫酷产品，以及在工作以外建造东西。
      最终，您作为产品经理负责运送成功的产品。帮助您的企业和客户成功。
  在事物之上。提供结果。这些是出色的PM的属性，很少有人会不同意。
        Brandon Chu的产品管理第一原则。归根结底，产品管理可以最大程度地提高对任务的影响，并提供一系列投入，并通过其他方面完成所有工作。
  Alex Reeve的“伟大产品经理原则”，为产品经理汇集了22条原则（涵盖6个类别）。
刘易斯·林的ESTEEM方法：执行力，出色的沟通能力，战术意识，非凡的心理韧性，出色的团队建设者，Moonshot视野。
note_en:
It is a difficult question to answer because there are so many things that "great" product managers do. And because the discipline itself is changing very quickly. There is no universal answer. But it is a question worth asking.
  I tackled this question by interviewing industry leaders, drawing on my own experience, and research.
  Here are the top traits, principles, and tactics I collected, that great product managers tend to share.
      As product managers, we face many decisions every day. But what does "effective decision making" mean?
  Being "data-driven" is a good start. But the term is so overused that it has lost its meaning. It also has its blind spots.
    Great product managers are able to find the right balance between data and intuition at any given moment to inform and drive their decision making. This could be something as small a bug fix, or something much larger such as which features to include in a v1 vs what to cut.The best product managers I've worked with are able to do this very well, balancing all sorts of inputs of "data" and combining it with their intuition to find the right balance of any given moment on what to index on.
      Both scientists and product managers play a truth-seeking game — scientists test their ideas in the natural world; product managers test theirs in the market.
  ﻿   Daniel Falabella  , Director of Product at Duolingo, believes that "90% of a Product Manager’s job is to find 'truth'". And this is one reason why Daniel thinks that entrepreneurs make the best PMs. He added,
  Lots of PMs go through the motions: they talk to teammates, become familiar with vanity metrics, talk to 2 users, or assume that their managers are right. They give the illusion of having the right inputs, only to pay up the price when it's time to discuss outputs.
      Product managers are responsible for the success of their products. But the path to get there is rarely straight-forward.
    Great PMs are adaptable. They know when to go into execution mode, and when to step back and work on strategy. They make everyone around them feel heard. At the end of the day, they make their customers and business successful, and their teams and cross functional partners want to work with them over and over again.
    ﻿   Erik Torenberg  , co-founder at Village Global and On Deck,    talked about a similar idea for founders  :
  Underrated skill for founders: Altitude shifting. People are often either good at high-level strategy or atomic-level execution, but rarely both.It's the ability to zoom out & paint the 5 yr vision, and then drop down into the weeds of day-to-day, & see how the two connect.
      ﻿   Brandon Chu  , VP Product & GM of Platform at Shopify,    wrote in an article   that some of the best PMs he knows make their decisions based on first principles. He explained,
  First principle thinking helps PMs because as companies scale, communicating the rationale behind historical, current, and future decisions can be simplified in a way that their team and stakeholders can rally around.
  This enables people around the PM to move quickly in the same direction, decouple, and make smart trade offs without their presence.
          It is tempting, especially for the technically-minded folks, to start with an idea for a solution and run with it. But building a great product requires understanding the problem, and understanding the person behind the problem.
  ﻿   Bo Ren  , Head of Ecosystem and Assistant GM at Samsung NEXT,    explained in an interview  ,
  If you get on the ground and hear what people are suffering from, then you can have a deeper understanding of what needs to be done. It’s not just empathy. It’s being specific and zoning in on the areas of improvement based on people’s real experiences."
    ﻿   Melissa Perri  ,   CEO and founder of Produx Labs,    said in an interview   that "what sets a decent product manager apart from a really great product manager, is really the way they think and approach problems". She continued,
  To me, thinking like a product manager is about problem solving. It’s about synthesizing a lot of information, understanding the system, trying to piece together what is the problem, breaking it down into small manageable chunks so you can analyze it, and then figuring out what is the right solution from there.
      Being humble/coachable is what Daniel (from earlier) considers the other important trait of a great product manager.
  Related to the truth-seeking point, you will be wrong a lot throughout your career. This may be a tough pill to swallow for the smart and ambitious ones. But it is an important part of growing as a person and learning to make better decisions over time.
      Your work isn't done when the requirements are defined, or even when the product gets shipped.
    If you’re not sick of saying it, you probably aren’t saying it enough. Constant communication might feel like “fluff,” but it isn’t. Evangelism is a critical part of the role—and it’s your job to make sure the organization is aligned and swimming in the same direction.
    For you [product managers], communication   is   a primary “output,” and it should be exceptional.
      There are always more things to build than there is time for. And often, you don't get as many people working on your product as you would like.
    This could mean finding cheaper technical workarounds. This could mean ruthlessly focusing on the small number of things that will really move the needle.
      ﻿   Ellen Chisa  , Co-founder of Dark and former VP of Product at Lola,    said in an interview   that, "You have to really love making things, but you have to be okay with not being the person actually making the thing."
  Having a genuine love for making things is powerful. This intrinsic motivation can keep you going, especially when things get difficult.
  This genuine interest also translates to you noticing other cool products in your life and building things outside of work.
      Ultimately, your responsibility as a product manager to ship successful products. To help your business and customers succeed.
  Being on top of things. Delivering results. These are attributes of a great PM that few would disagree.
        ﻿   The First Principles of Product Management   by Brandon Chu. It boils down product management to maximizing impact to the mission given a set of inputs and accomplishing everything through others.
  ﻿   Principles for Great Product Managers   by Alex Reeve, piecing together 22 principles (across 6 categories) for product managers.
  ﻿   ESTEEM Method   by Lewis C. Lin: Execution, Superior communication skills, Tactical awareness, Extraordinary mental toughness, Exceptional team builder, Moonshot vision.

焦糖具有高表现力，声音类型系统和快速的类型检查器。这使您可以通过使用更接近您的域的类型来排除整个错误类别，并在不牺牲开发速度的情况下毫不费力地重构大量代码。
  为了实现这一目标，Caramel实际上是OCaml编译器的后端，因此，它重用了整个类型检查堆栈，并进行了25年以上的研究和数百万小时的学术研究和工业使用，并为Erlang提供了新的编译目标。
      Caramel在Erlang虚拟机上运行，​​该虚拟机可能是世界上最成熟，最可靠的功能语言运行时。它具有内置的错误处理机制，非常适合构建永不停止运行，需要实时处理大量并发并且仅由少数工程师构建的系统。
note_en:
Caramel has a  highly expressive, sound type-system, and a blazing fast type-checker. This allows you to rule out entire classes of errors by using types that are closer to your domain, and fearlessly refactor large amounts of code without sacrificing development speed.
  To achieve this, Caramel is actually  a backend to the OCaml compiler, so it reuses its entire type-checking stack, with well over 25 years and millions of hours of academic research and industrial usage, and provides a new compilation target to Erlang.
      Caramel runs on the  Erlang virtual machine, which is perhaps the most mature and reliable functional language runtime in the world. It has built-in error handling mechanisms that make it ideal for building systems that should never stop running, that need to handle massive concurrency in soft-real time, and that need to be built by just a handful of engineers.
title: 焦糖色：OCaml到Erlang编译器
title_en: Caramel: An OCaml to Erlang Compiler

首先，事实证明，SAT并不能衡量独立于社会和教育背景的学术才能或本地智力。相反，SAT分数与财富高度相关。您的家庭收入越高，您的SAT分数就越高。在收入阶梯上的每个连续梯级，平均SAT分数都会提高。对于那些让学生争夺最挑剔的大学的分数，差距尤为明显。如果您来自一个年收入超过200,000美元的家庭，则得分高于1400（1600分）的机会是五分之一。如果您来自一个贫穷的家庭（每年少于20,000美元），则机会是十分之一。高分类别的人绝大多数也是拥有大学学位的父母的孩子。
 除了小康家庭可以提供的一般教育优势外，通过使用私人备考课程和家教，可以提高特权学生的SAT分数。有些在曼哈顿等地，一对一补习每小时收取高达1,000美元的费用。近几十年来，随着大学录取的精英竞争日益激烈，补习和考试准备已成为一个十亿美元的产业。
  多年来，负责管理SAT的大学理事会一直坚持认为它的测验可以衡量能力，并且分数不会受到补习的影响。它最近放弃了这种假装，并与可汗学院建立了合作伙伴关系，向所有应试者提供免费的在线SAT练习。尽管这是一项有价值的工作，但它并没有像大学委员会的官员所希望并声称的那样，为准备考试做好了准备。也许不足为奇的是，收入和文化程度较高的家庭的学生比处境不利的学生更多地使用在线帮助，从而导致特权与其他人之间的得分差距更大。
 对于美国化学家，哈佛大学前任校长詹姆斯·布莱恩特·科南特（James Bryant Conant）而言，能力测验或智商测验有望成为衡量学业能力的一种民主手段，不受教育劣势和高中成绩变化的影响。这就是为什么他选择SAT选择奖学金学生的原因。如果发现高中成绩比SAT成绩更好地识别了可能在大学获得成功的低收入学生，他会感到惊讶。
 高中成绩在某种程度上与家庭收入相关，SAT分数则更高。部分原因是，与测试行业的长期主张相反，SAT是可指导的。私人补习会有所帮助，并且已经出现了一个赚钱的行业，可以教高中生students头和技巧来提高他们的成绩。
       其次，科南特倡导的精英管理制度并没有导致他希望它将产生的无阶级社会。自1940年代和1950年代以来，收入和财富的不平等现象加剧了，而科南特（Conant）认为这是对分层社会的一种补救措施，并没有实现社会流动性。富人和富人没有从一代到下一代进行交易。正如我们所看到的那样，穷人的富裕子女相对较少，而富裕的子女则属于中上阶层以下。尽管美国人梦想着从破烂到富裕，但向上流动在美国并不像许多欧洲国家那么普遍，而且最近几十年没有改善的迹象。
  哈佛，耶鲁和普林斯顿长期以来都偏爱上层寄宿学校的年轻人，这种偏爱在1960年代和1970年代逐渐消失。任何有最低学历的校友儿子的日常入读常春藤盟校的大学也是如此。学术水平提高，SAT分数中位数提高。得天独厚的大学采用盲目录取和慷慨的财政援助政策，为有前途的中等收入学生消除了主要的经济障碍。
这些是不可否认的成就。然而，高等教育的精英革命并没有带来早期支持者所期望的社会流动性和广泛的机会，而教育领袖和政治家仍在继续承诺。美国的精选大学驱逐了让科纳特感到担忧的自满，有称职的世袭精英。但是，这种继承特权的贵族制已经让给了一个精英阶层的精英阶层，如今，精英阶层的精英地位和地位已与其取代的精英阶层一样牢固。
 尽管在性别，种族和族裔方面更具包容性，但这种精英精英阶层并没有产生一个流动的，流动的社会。取而代之的是，今天有证书的专业班级已经弄清楚了如何将特权传递给孩子们，而不是给孩子留下大庄园，而是给孩子们提供了决定精英社会成功的优势。
  尽管高等教育是机会的仲裁者和向上流动的引擎，但它的新发现是，它并没有为近来日益加剧的不平等现象提供重要的平衡作用。考虑今天的高等教育的班级构成，尤其是在最挑剔的领域：
 精选大学的大多数学生来自富裕家庭。来自极低收入背景的人很少。参加美国100所最具竞争力的大学的学生中，有70％以上来自收入规模的前四分之一；只有3％来自最底层的季度。
 大学入学率的贫富差距最严重。在常春藤盟校，斯坦福大学，杜克大学和其他享有声望的地方，来自最富有的1％家庭的学生比来自整个美国下半部的学生还要多。在耶鲁大学和普林斯顿大学，只有约五十分之一的学生来自一个贫穷的家庭（最低20％）。
 如果您来自一个富裕的家庭（收入最高的1％），那么您上常春藤联盟学校的机会比来自一个贫穷的家庭（最低20％）的机会要高77倍。收入水平最低的一半的大多数年轻人上两年制大学，或者根本不上。
     在过去的二十年中，精英私立大学提供了更为慷慨的财政援助，而联邦政府也增加了对中等收入学生的大学资助。例如，哈佛和斯坦福大学现在向家庭年收入低于65,000美元的任何学生免费提供学费，食宿。尽管采取了这些措施，但是自2000年以来，精选大学中来自低收入家庭的学生所占比例几乎没有变化，在某些情况下一直在下降。今天，哈佛“第一代”学生（家庭中第一位上大学）的比例并不比1960年高。杰罗姆·卡拉贝尔（Jerome Karabel）是哈佛，耶鲁和普林斯顿大学录取政策史的作者。 “工人阶级的孩子和穷人的孩子今天不大可能像1954年那样参加三巨头（哈佛，耶鲁和普林斯顿）。”
美国领先的学院和大学的学术声誉，科学贡献和丰富的教育产品享誉全球。但是这些机构并不是向上流动的有效引擎。最近，经济学家拉吉·切蒂（Raj Chetty）和一组同事对大学在促进代际流动方面的作用进行了全面研究，研究了1999年至2013年3000万大学生的经济发展轨迹。他们针对美国的每一所大学计算了从收入阶梯的最底层到最上层（即，从最低的五分之一到最高的五分之一）上升的学生比例。换句话说，他们问每所大学中有多少比例的学生来自贫困家庭，但收入却足以达到前20％。他们的发现：如今，高等教育对于促进向上流动性的作用令人惊讶。
 在精英私立大学中尤其如此。尽管去哈佛或普林斯顿这样的地方确实能给贫穷的孩子一个成长的机会，但这类地方招收的贫穷孩子很少，所以他们的流动性很低。哈佛学生中只有1.8％（普林斯顿大学只有1.3％）从收入范围的底部升至最高。人们可能会期望主要的公立大学有所不同。但是他们也招收了许多已经很富裕的学生，以至于他们对向上流动性的贡献很小。总体而言，切蒂研究的1800所大学（私立和公立，有选择的和非有选择的）使不到2％的学生从收入范围的后五名升至前五名。有些人可能会问，是否推动一代一代的学生从最低的五分之一（家庭收入不超过20,000美元）到最高的（收入110,000美元或更高）的学生对流动性的要求过高。但是，相对温和的攀登却相对罕见。在精英私立学院和大学中，只有十分之一的学生设法在收入阶梯上甚至提高了两个梯级（两个五分位数）。
 尽管进入这样的地方确实可以提高一个人的经济前景，但美国的大学和大学却很少能使学生的崛起。大学毕业生，尤其是来自享有盛誉的地方的毕业生，在获得丰厚的工作方面确实具有很大优势。但是这些学校对向上流动性影响不大，因为大多数学生一开始都富裕。美国高等教育就像是大多数人进入顶层的大楼中的电梯。
    实际上，大多数高校在扩大机会方面没有做任何事情来巩固特权。对于那些将高等教育视为机遇的主要工具的人来说，这是一个令人震惊的新闻。它引起了人们对当代政治信仰的质疑，即对不平等现象日益严重的答案是更大的流动性，而增加流动性的方法就是让更多的人上大学。
   摘录自迈克尔·桑德尔（Michael J. Sandel）的《功绩暴政》（The Tyranny of Merit），经企鹅兰登豪斯（Penguin Randomhouse）特殊许可出版。

ThePrint拥有最好的年轻记者，专栏作家和编辑。要保持这种质量的新闻业，就需要聪明而有思想的人，像您一样为之付出代价。无论您居住在印度还是国外，都可以在这里进行。

  因此，麻省理工学院，加州理工学院和芝加哥大学录取学生的素质更高。

note_en:
First, the SAT, it turns out, does not measure scholarly aptitude or native intelligence independent of social and educational background. To the contrary, SAT scores are highly correlated with wealth. The higher your family income, the higher your SAT score. At each successive rung on the income ladder, average SAT scores increase. For scores that put students in contention for the most selective colleges, the gap is especially stark. If you come from a family with an annual income greater than $200,000, your chance of scoring above 1400 (out of 1600) is one in five. If you come from a poor family (less than $20,000 per year), your chance is one in fifty. Those in high-scoring categories are also, overwhelmingly, children of parents with college degrees.
 Beyond the general educational advantages well-off families can provide, the SAT scores of the privileged are boosted by the use of private test- prep courses and tutors. Some, in places like Manhattan, charge as much as $1,000 per hour for one-on-one tutoring. As meritocratic competition for college admission has intensified in recent decades, tutoring and test prep has become a billion-dollar industry.
  For years, the College Board, which administers the SAT, insisted that its test measured aptitude and that scores were unaffected by tutoring. It recently dropped that pretense and entered a partnership with the Khan Academy to provide free online SAT practice to all test takers. Although this was a worthy undertaking, it did little to level the test-prep playing field, as College Board officials hoped and claimed it would. Unsurprisingly perhaps, students from families with higher incomes and education levels made greater use of the online help than did students from disadvantaged backgrounds, resulting in an even greater scoring gap between the privileged and the rest.
 For James Bryant Conant, an American chemist and former president of Harvard University, a test of aptitude or IQ held promise as a democratic measure of academic ability, untainted by educational disadvantage and the vagaries of high school grades. This is why he opted for the SAT to choose his scholarship students. He would be surprised to learn that high school grades are better than SAT scores at identifying low-income students who are likely to succeed in college.
 While high school grades are to some extent correlated with family income, SAT scores are more so. This is partly because, contrary to long-standing claims by the testing industry, the SAT is coachable. Private tutoring helps, and a profitable industry has arisen to teach high school students the gimmicks and tricks to boost their scores.
       Second, the system of meritocratic admission that Conant promoted did not lead to the classless society he hoped it would produce. Inequalities of income and wealth have deepened since the 1940s and 1950s, and the social mobility that Conant saw as the remedy for a stratified society has not come about. The haves and have-nots have not been trading places from one generation to the next. As we have seen, relatively few children of the poor rise to affluence, and relatively few children of affluence fall below the ranks of the upper-middle class. Notwithstanding the American dream of rising from rags to riches, upward mobility is less common in the United States than in many European countries, and there is no evidence of improvement in recent decades.
  The favoritism that Harvard, Yale, and Princeton long accorded young men from upper-class boarding schools receded in the 1960s and 1970s. So did the routine admission to Ivy League colleges of any minimally qualified son of an alumnus. Academic standards improved and median SAT scores increased. The best-endowed colleges and universities adopted need-blind admissions and generous financial-aid policies, removing a major financial barrier for promising students of modest means.
  These are the undeniable achievements. And yet, the meritocratic revolution in higher education did not bring about the social mobility and broad opportunity that its early proponents expected and that educational leaders and politicians continue to promise. America’s selective colleges and universities ousted the complacent, entitled, hereditary elite that worried Conant. But this aristocracy of inherited privilege has given way to a meritocratic elite that is now as privileged and entrenched as the one it replaced.
 Though far more inclusive in terms of gender, race, and ethnicity, this meritocratic elite has not produced a fluid, mobile society. Instead, today’s credentialed, professional classes have figured out how to pass their privileges on to their children, not by bequeathing them large estates but by equipping them with the advantages that determine success in a meritocratic society.
  Notwithstanding its newfound role as the arbiter of opportunity and the engine of upward mobility, higher education has not provided a significant counterweight to the rising inequality of recent times. Consider the class composition of higher education today, especially in its most selective domains:
 Most students at selective colleges and universities are from affluent families; very few are from low-income backgrounds. More than 70 percent of those who attend the hundred or so most competitive colleges in the United States come from the top quarter of the income scale; only 3 percent come from the bottom quarter.
 The wealth gap in college enrollment is most acute at the top. At Ivy League colleges, Stanford, Duke, and other prestigious places, there are more students from the wealthiest 1 percent of families than from the entire bottom half of the country. At Yale and Princeton, only about one student in fifty comes from a poor family (bottom 20 percent).
 If you come from a rich family (top 1 percent), your chances of attending an Ivy League school are 77 times greater than if you come from a poor family (bottom 20 percent). Most young people from the bottom half of the income scale attend a two-year college or none at all.
     Over the last two decades, elite private colleges have offered more generous financial aid, and the federal government has increased college funding for students of modest means. Harvard and Stanford, for example, now provide free tuition, room, and board to any student whose family makes less than $65,000 per year. Despite these measures, however, the share of students from low-income families at selective colleges has changed little since 2000 and in some cases has drifted downward. The percentage of “first generation” students (the first in their families to attend college) at Harvard today is no higher than it was in 1960. Jerome Karabel, the author of a history of admissions policies at Harvard, Yale, and Princeton, concludes that “the children of the working class and the poor are about as unlikely to attend the Big Three [Harvard, Yale, and Princeton] today as they were in 1954.”
  The academic reputations, scientific contributions, and rich educational offerings of America’s leading colleges and universities are admired throughout the world. But these institutions are not effective engines of upward mobility. Recently, the economist Raj Chetty and a team of colleagues undertook a comprehensive study of the role of colleges in promoting intergenerational mobility, examining the economic trajectory of 30 million college students from 1999 to 2013. For each college in the United States, they calculated the proportion of its students who rose from the bottom rung on the income ladder to the top (i.e., from the bottom quintile to the top quintile). They asked, in other words, what proportion of students at each college came from a poor family but wound up earning enough to reach the top 20 percent. Their finding: higher education today does surprisingly little to promote upward mobility.
 This is especially true at elite private colleges. Although attending a place like Harvard or Princeton does give a poor kid a good chance of rising, such places enroll so few poor kids to begin with that their mobility rate is low. Only 1.8 percent of Harvard students (and only 1.3 percent at Princeton) rise from the bottom to the top of the income scale. One might expect things to be different at the major public universities. But they, too, enroll so many already-affluent students that they contribute little to upward mobility. Taken together, the 1,800 colleges and universities Chetty studied—private and public, selective and non-selective—enabled fewer than 2 percent of their students to rise from the bottom fifth of the income scale to the top fifth. Some might ask whether propelling students, in one generation, from the bottom quintile ($20,000 family income or less) to the top ($110,000 income or more) is too demanding a test of mobility. But even more modest ascents are relatively rare. At elite private colleges and universities, only about one student in ten manages to rise even two rungs (two quintiles) on the income ladder.
 American colleges and universities enable surprisingly few students to rise, despite the fact that attending such places does enhance one’s economic prospects. College graduates, especially from prestigious places, do have a major edge in landing lucrative jobs. But these schools have little impact on upward mobility, because most of their students are well-off in the first place. American higher education is like an elevator in a building that most people enter on the top floor.
    In practice, most colleges and universities do less to expand opportunity than to consolidate privilege. For those who look to higher education as the primary vehicle of opportunity, this is sobering news. It calls into question an article of faith in contemporary politics—that the answer to rising inequality is greater mobility, and that the way to increase mobility is to send more people to college.
   This excerpt from The Tyranny of Merit  by Michael J. Sandel has been published with special permission from Penguin Randomhouse.

title: 高收入而不是SAT成绩是您进入精英大学的真正入场券
title_en: High family income, not SAT scores, is your real ticket to elite university

note:
2020年是具有历史意义的一年，而且大部分情况都不佳。在许多方面，我们看到了对COVID-19病毒无视科学建议的历史性水平，这种无视使大流行在美国比其他许多国家更严重。但是，尽管2020年的事件可能让人感到空前，但拒绝科学证据的社会格局在今年并未突然出现。从来没有任何良好的科学理由拒绝关于COVID的专家建议，就像从来没有任何良好的科学理由怀疑人类在进化，疫苗可以挽救生命，以及温室气体正在推动破坏性的气候变化一样。要了解拒绝科学发现和专家建议的社会模式，我们需要将科学超越历史，这告诉我们，拒绝专家证据和传播虚假信息的多种形式都源于烟草的历史。
  在整个20世纪上半叶，大多数美国人将科学视为使我们的生活更加美好的事物。科学加深了我们对自然世界的理解，这有助于我们治愈疾病，照亮房屋并为我们的生活带来新的娱乐方式。也许最重要的是，物理学家帮助赢得了第二次世界大战并成为文化英雄。化学家也应得的。正如杜邦（DuPont）提醒我们的那样，我们拥有“更好的东西，可以通过化学更好地生活”。在通用电气，科学家和工程师正在帮助“将美好的事物带入生活”。这些不仅仅是口号；公司研发确实确实生产了可改善许多美国人生活的产品。但是美国公司也在开发有关科学否定和虚假信息的剧本。
  这个黑暗故事的罪魁祸首是烟草业，科学，技术和医学的历史学家以及流行病学家和律师对烟草业的剧本进行了充分的记录。它通过提倡一种观念来贬低科学，这种观念认为吸烟与肺癌和其他疾病之间的联系是不确定的或不完整的，而对其进行调节的尝试是对美国自由的威胁。该行业通过增加其尼古丁含量而使产品更容易上瘾，同时公开否认尼古丁会上瘾。有了这些策略，在明显的危害性科学证据明确之后，烟草业就能够推迟有效的措施来阻止吸烟。在我们2010年的著作《怀疑的商人》中，埃里克·康威（Erik M. Conway）和我展示了如何使用相同的论据来延迟对酸雨，臭氧空洞和气候变化的行动-今年，我们看到了虚假的“自由”论据戴口罩。
  我们还看到了烟草战略正在渗透到社交媒体中，这影响了公众舆论，许多人认为烟草战略需要受到更多的审查，甚至可能受到政府监管。 2019年10月，国会举行了听证会，以调查Facebook在可能传播错误信息中的作用。 2020年夏天，民权律师事务所Relman Colfax的一份报告建议，Facebook帖子可能有助于压制选民。气候科学家抱怨说，这家社交媒体巨头通过允许虚假或误导性声明，同时在主流科学家的标签上贴上“政治”标签，阻碍了他们的回应，从而助长了气候否认活动。
  没有历史的眼光，我们可能会将其解释为由新技术创造的新问题。但是今年9月，一位前Facebook经理在国会作证说，该公司“借鉴了Big Tobacco的剧本，致力于使我们的产品上瘾”，他说Facebook决心让人们对其产品上瘾，同时公开使用其委婉语。增加“参与度”。像烟草业一样，社交媒体公司向我们出售了一种有毒产品，同时坚持认为它只是在给消费者提供他们想要的东西。
  科学界的同事经常问我为什么将科学职业换成历史职业。对于其中一些人来说，历史只是“过去的存在”。但是，正如吟游诗人在《暴风雨》中所说：“过去就是序幕。”如果我们要面对虚假信息，对科学发现的拒绝以及对技术的负面使用，我们必须了解将我们带到这一点的过去。”
note_en:
2020 has been a historic year— and mostly not in a good way. Among many things, we saw a historic level of disregard of scientific advice with respect to the COVID-19 virus, a disregard that made the pandemic worse in the U.S. than in many other countries. But while the events of 2020 may feel unprecedented, the social pattern of rejecting scientific evidence did not suddenly appear this year. There was never any good scientific reason for rejecting the expert advice on COVID, just as there has never been any good scientific reason for doubting that humans evolved, that vaccines save lives, and that greenhouse gases are driving disruptive climate change. To understand the social pattern of rejecting scientific findings and expert advice, we need to look beyond science to history, which tells us that many of the various forms of the rejection of expert evidence and the promotion of disinformation have roots in the history of tobacco.
  Throughout the first half of the 20th century, most Americans saw science as something that made our lives better. Science had deepened our understanding of the natural world, which helped us to cure diseases, light our homes and bring new forms of entertainment into our lives. Perhaps most important, physicists helped to win World War II and became cultural heroes. Chemists got their due, too. As DuPont reminded us, we had  “better things for better living through chemistry.” At General Electric, scientists and engineers were helping to  “bring good things to life.” These were not just slogans; corporate R&D really did produce products that measurably improved many American lives. But corporate America was also developing the playbook for science denial and disinformation.
  The chief culprit in this darker story was the tobacco industry, whose playbook has been well documented by historians of  science,  technology and  medicine, as well as  epidemiologists and  lawyers. It disparaged science by promoting the idea that the link between tobacco use and lung cancer and other diseases was uncertain or incomplete and that the attempt to regulate it was a threat to American freedom. The industry made products more addictive by increasing their nicotine content while  publicly denying that nicotine was addictive. With these tactics, the industry was able to delay effective measures to discourage smoking long after the scientific evidence of its harms was clear. In our 2010 book,  Merchants of Doubt, Erik M. Conway and I showed how the same arguments were used to delay action on acid rain, the ozone hole and climate change—and this year we saw the spurious “freedom” argument being used to disparage mask wearing.
  We also saw the tobacco strategy seeping into social media, which influences public opinion and which many people feel needs to be subject to greater scrutiny and perhaps  government regulation. In October 2019 Congress held hearings to investigate the role of Facebook in potentially spreading misinformation. In the summer of 2020 a  report from civil-rights law firm  Relman Colfax suggested that Facebook posts could contribute to voter suppression. Climate scientists have complained that the social media giant contributes to the spread of climate denial by permitting false or misleading claims while hobbling responses by mainstream scientists by labeling their posts “political.”
  Without a historical perspective, we might interpret this as a novel problem created by a novel technology. But this past September, a former Facebook manager  testified in Congress that the company “took a page from Big Tobacco's playbook, working to make our offering addictive,” saying that Facebook was determined to make people addicted to its products while publicly using the euphemism of increasing “engagement.” Like the tobacco industry, social media companies sold us a toxic product while insisting that it was simply giving consumers what they wanted.
  Scientific colleagues often ask me why I traded a career in science for a career in history. History, for some of them, is just “dwelling on the past.” But, as the bard said in  The Tempest: “What's past is prologue.” If we are to confront disinformation, the rejection of scientific findings, and the negative uses of technology, we have to understand the past that has brought us to this point.”
title: 了解科学否定的原理，回顾历史
title_en: To Understand How Science Denial Works, Look to History

note: 要掌握预测世界倒置后将发生什么的困难程度（就像今年一样），您必须知道关于现代历史上最重要的农业发展之一是如何成为荒谬的故事。它始于1815年，印度尼西亚的坦波拉火山爆发。坦波拉（Tambora）是一场彻底的灾难-了解得越多，您就越会意识到很少有形容词就足够了。它是人类历史上最大的火山喷发，比圣海伦斯火山（Mount St. Helens）强100倍。一万当地人立即死亡。在爆炸中幸存下来的大多数人，在Sumbawa岛上几乎所有植被被摧毁后，都死于饥饿。然后是真正的灾难。火山喷发柱冲向天空26英里，将碎片推入平流层深处-这是人类历史上发生过的少数几次。而且还有大量的碎片：坦波拉弹出了足够的灰烬，足以覆盖加利福尼亚州两英尺深的火山灰。然后，灰烬流到了全球各地，造成了严重破坏。您可能已经看到了今年夏天旧金山的照片，当时野火的烟雾和灰烬暂时使天空变成深红色。坦波拉（Tambora）在世界大部分地区都做到了这一点。它做了几个月。这样就开始了所谓的“没有夏天的一年”。太阳被灰烬阻挡，1816年全球平均气温下降1.5度，是有史以来最冷的一年。欧洲在整个7月都下雪，大部分降雪不是白色而是灰橙色，还有灰烬。纽约在八月结霜。新英格兰人开始将这一年称为“一百八十岁而死”。低温和低日照成为农业灾难。作物在世界各地失败。饥荒接followed而至。在拿破仑战争之后一直苦苦挣扎的欧洲受到的打击尤其严重。德国的情况最糟。饥饿和粮价飞涨使绝望的，动荡的人口变成骚乱者。一位德国市长写道：许多人感到通货膨胀和饥饿，以至于他们和他们的孩子生存下来，他们用所谓的猪耳朵煮蜗牛和煮熟的白菜，然后吃掉它们，使他们变得疲软，常常无法更长的工作时间，甚至更糟的是无法行走，并且由于饥饿，他们的脚肿胀，头部肿胀。其中一名饥饿的德国人是一个13岁的男孩，名叫Justus von Liebig。利比希（Liebig）是一个不起眼的孩子，在学校被贴上了“绝望无用”的标签。但是他喜欢修补和试验。 Liebig的父亲是一名油漆制造商，并将这个男孩介绍给了他所钟爱的工业化学。 Justus von Liebig随后决定了两件事。一个是他想成为一名化学家。他21岁时获得博士学位。另一位则更具野心：1816年饥荒期间的经验深深影响着他，他将化学事业投身于改善农业。这正是他所做的。利比希（Liebig）是最早了解氮对植物健康和农作物产量的重要性以及如何使用氨人工供应氮的机理的人之一。他还推广了最低限度法则，即植物营养素总量不重要的想法；一种稀缺的养分会降低产量，而养分通常是氮。在大自然母亲没有合作的几年里，氨是使产量最大化和健康作物生长所需的缺失成分。像1816年一样。李比希（Liebig）的工作（在随后的几十年中得到了改进，特别是通过学习如何合成氨的科学家的研究得到了改进），使氨基化肥产业得以占据全球农业的主导地位。很难夸大这笔交易有多大。瓦茨拉夫·斯米尔（Vaclav Smil）在他的《丰富地球：氨的工业合成中》……对现代世界而言，比飞机，核能，太空飞行或电视的发明更重要。如果没有合成氨，世界人口不可能从1900年的16亿增加到今天的60亿。在任何情况下，Liebig的故事都是令人印象深刻的。但是，当您问一个问题时，它会令人着迷：他是否会从事化学工作，专注于农业并且是否发现了氮肥？坦波拉（Tambora）在7,000英里外的喷发导致了德国的饥荒？可能不会。哪个很疯狂。火山爆炸，然后另一个大陆的孩子受到启发，找出如何更有效地喂养地球。没有任何一个理性的人会在1816年建立这种联系。但这就是发生的事情。里比希并不孤单。坦波拉（Tambora）的瘟疫为运动开辟了无意的道路。在饥荒期间饲料价格飙升之后，寻找马的替代品，德国发明家卡尔·冯·德莱斯（Karl von Drais）发明了自行车。从新英格兰到中原的移民激增，而中原平原受塔博拉火山灰的影响较小。历史学家劳伦斯·戈德曼（Lawrence Goldman）认为，1816年以农作物为驱动力的移民到纽约西部对于巩固反奴隶制运动至关重要。所有这些都由令人敬畏的火山煽动。哪个使您感到奇怪：2020年令人敬畏的病毒将通过哪些意外路径启动？进化生物学中有一种理论称为费舍尔自然选择基础定理。这就是差异等于力量的想法，因为人口越多样化，就越有机会提出可以选择的新特征。没有人知道什么特质会有用。这不是进化的原理。但是，如果您创造了很多特质，那么有用的特质-无论它是什么-都会存在于某个地方。同一件事适用于社会面临的各种事件。很难想象Covid-19的上升空间，即使不是鲁still的。我们可能还没有看到最糟糕的情况。但是，世界上每个人突然都面临着从未见过的问题。他们已经意识到新的风险。他们的生活，工作和娱乐方式受到新的限制。有关如何保护家人安全，开展业务和使用技术的全新观点。将会带来的一些变化是显而易见的。与一年前相比，我们在制造疫苗方面已经做得更好，更快。医生知识渊博。远程工作效率更高。旅行是不必要的。然后是第二层变革：也许利用我们对mRNA疫苗的新知识来治疗其他疾病，例如癌症。似乎有可能，但谁知道。然后是一个巨大的未知数：疯狂的，脱节的，违反直觉的变化在今年开始发生，我们只能事后相识。只有当70亿人的生活被颠倒，经历了许多他们从未想象过的事情，或者被激励或被迫做与一月份所想的完全不同的事情时，这些事情才会发生。没有人甚至应该猜测可能是什么。不可预测性是重点。但是，当本周好的疫苗新闻出来时，好几个人说：“隧道尽头有光明。”也许。但是我怀疑我们不知道接下来会发生什么。
note_en: To grasp how hard it is to predict what will happen after the world is thrown upside down – as it has been this year – you have to know the absurd story of how one of the most important agricultural developments of modern history came to be. It started in 1815 when the volcano Mt. Tambora in Indonesia erupted. Tambora was an utter disaster – the more you read about it the more you realize few adjectives suffice. It was the largest volcanic eruption in human history, 100 times more powerful than Mount St. Helens. Ten thousand locals died instantly. Most of those who survived the blast went on to die of starvation after nearly all vegetation on the island of Sumbawa was destroyed. Then came the real catastrophe. The volcanic eruption column surged 26 miles into the sky, pushing debris deep into the stratosphere – one of only a handful of times it’s happened in human history. And there was an epic amount of debris: Tambora ejected enough ash to cover the equivalent of California two-feet deep. Ash then drifted around the globe, where it caused havoc. You’ve probably seen pictures of San Francisco this summer when smoke and ash from wildfires temporarily turned the sky dark crimson. Tambora did that to much of the world. And it did it for several months. Thus began what became known as The Year Without A Summer. The sun blocked by ash, global temperatures fell an average of 1.5 degrees in 1816, marking the coldest year of recorded history. Europe saw snow through July, much of it coming down not white but greyish orange, mixed with ash. New York saw frost in August. New Englanders began referring to the year as Eighteen Hundred and Froze to Death. Freezing temperatures and low sunlight became an agricultural disaster. Crops failed throughout the world. Famine followed. Europe – already struggling in the aftermath of the Napoleonic wars – was hit particularly hard. Germany had it worst. Hunger and surging food prices turned a desperate, agitated population into rioters. A German mayor wrote: The inflation and hunger was felt so hard by many that in order for them and their children to survive, they boiled snails and cooked green cabbage from so called pig’s ears and ate them, whereby they became enfeebled and often could no longer work, and even worse could not walk, and as a result of the hunger their feet swelled and their heads swelled. One of those starving Germans was a 13-year-old boy named Justus von Liebig. Liebig was an unremarkable child, labeled in school as “hopelessly useless.” But he liked to tinker and experiment. Liebig’s father was a paint manufacturer, and introduced the boy to industrial chemistry, which he fell in love with. Justus von Liebig then decided two things. One was that he wanted to become a chemist. He received his PhD when he was 21 years old. The other was more ambitious: Deeply affected by his experience during the famine of 1816, he would devote his chemistry career to improving agriculture. Which is exactly what he did. Liebig was one of the first to understand the mechanics of how important nitrogen was to plant health and crop yield, and how ammonia could be used to supply it artificially. He also popularized the law of the minimum, or the idea that the total amount of plant nutrients wasn’t important; a single scarce nutrient could depress yields, and that nutrient was usually nitrogen. Ammonia was the missing ingredient needed to maximize yields and grow healthy crops during years Mother Nature didn’t cooperate. Like in 1816. Liebig’s work – improved upon in the following decades, particularly by scientists who learned how to manufacture ammonia synthetically – gave rise to the ammonia-based fertilizer industry that still dominates global agriculture. It’s hard to exaggerate how big a deal it’s been. Vaclav Smil writes in his book Enriching The Earth: The industrial synthesis of ammonia … has been of greater fundamental importance to the modern world than the invention of the airplane, nuclear energy, space flight, or television. The expansion of the world’s population from 1.6 billion people in 1900 to today’s six billion would not have been possible without the synthesis of ammonia. Liebig’s story is impressive in any context. But it’s fascinating when you ask the question: Would he have pursued a career in chemistry, focused on agriculture, and discovered nitrogen-based fertilizer if it weren’t for Mt. Tambora’s eruption 7,000 miles away that caused a German famine? Probably not. Which is wild. A volcano explodes, then a kid on a different continent becomes inspired to figure out how to feed the planet more efficiently. No reasonable person would make that connection in 1816. But it’s what happened. And Liebig wasn’t alone. Tambora’s plague set several unintentional paths in motion. Looking for an alternative to horses after feed prices surged during the famine, German inventor Karl von Drais invented the bicycle. Migration from New England to the central plains – which were less affected by Tambora’s ash – surged. Historian Lawrence Goldman argues that crop-driven migration into western New York in 1816 was critical to coalescing the anti-slavery movement. All instigated by a god-awful volcano. Which should make you wonder: What unintentional paths will 2020’s god-awful virus set in motion? There’s a theory in evolutionary biology called Fisher’s Fundamental Theorem of Natural Selection. It’s the idea that variance equals strength, because the more diverse a population is the more chances it has to come up with new traits that can be selected for. No one can know what traits will be useful; that’s not how evolution works. But if you create a lot of traits, the useful one – whatever it is – will be in there somewhere. The same thing applies to the diversity of events a society faces. It still feels hard, if not reckless, to imagine the upside of Covid-19. We may not have even seen the worst of it yet. But everyone in the world has suddenly been exposed to problems they had never seen before. They’ve become aware of new risks. New constraints in how they live, work, and play. A whole new set of perspectives on how to keep your family safe, run a business, and use technology. Some of the changes that will bring are obvious. We’re already better and faster at creating vaccines than we were a year ago. Doctors are more knowledgeable. Remote work is more efficient. Travel is less necessary. Then there’s a second tier of change: perhaps using our new knowledge of mRNA vaccines to treat other diseases, like cancer. It seems likely, but who knows. Then there’s the big unknown: the crazy, disconnected, counterintuitive change set in motion this year that we’ll only be able to piece together in hindsight. The kinds of things that only happen when seven billion people have their lives thrown upside down, experience a bunch of stuff they’d never imagined, and are either motivated or forced to do something completely different than they had considered in January. No one should even guess what that might be. The unpredictability is the point. But when good vaccine news came out this week, several people said, “there’s light at the end of the tunnel.” Maybe. But I suspect we have no idea what happens next.
title: 我们不知道接下来会发生什么
title_en: We Have No Idea What Happens Next

路透社）-美国最大的乐器和设备零售商吉它中心公司（Guitar Center Inc）于周六申请第11章破产保护，因为在冠状病毒大流行期间音乐爱好者将其购物转移到网上。
 该公司在refini.tv/3fpM2UC的一份声明中表示，该零售商已协商从其现有贷方获得总计3.75亿美元的债务人持有资产融资，并计划筹集3.35亿美元的新优先担保票据。
 本月早些时候，该公司与主要利益相关者达成了一项重组协议，其中包括减少近8亿美元的债务以及高达1.65亿美元的新股权投资，以对该公司进行资本重组。
  该公司在法庭文件中表示，其资产和负债均在10亿至100亿美元之间。
 在全国拥有近300家门店的吉它中心（Guitar Center）表示，业务运营将继续进行而不会受到任何干扰。
 Milbank LLP担任法律顾问，BRG担任重组顾问，Houlihan Lokey担任该公司的财务顾问。

note_en:
(Reuters) - Guitar Center Inc, the largest U.S. retailer of music instruments and equipment, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on Saturday, as music lovers moved their shopping online during the coronavirus pandemic.
 The retailer has negotiated to have a total of $375 million in debtor-in-possession financing from its existing lenders and intends to raise $335 million in new senior secured notes, the company said  refini.tv/3fpM2UC in a statement.
 Earlier this month the company reached a restructuring agreement with key stakeholders that includes debt reduction by nearly $800 million and new equity investments of up to $165 million to recapitalize the company.
  The company in a court filing said it has between $1 billion and $10 billion of both assets and liabilities.
 Guitar Center, which owns nearly 300 stores across the country, said business operations will continue without any interruption.
 Milbank LLP served as legal counsel, BRG served as restructuring advisor, and Houlihan Lokey was financial advisor to the company.
   The company filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in the United States Bankruptcy Court of the Eastern District of Virginia.
title: 美国零售商Guitar Center申请破产
title_en: U.S. retailer Guitar Center files for bankruptcy

科技巨头因两年前被告知平台上的极端主义活动而未能采取行动而受到批评
    Facebook和Instagram正在托管一个白人至上主义网络，该网络拥有超过80,000的追随者，并与英国最右端的链接，其中包括一名被控犯有恐怖主义罪行的学生，这使世界上最大的社交媒体公司承受着应对极端主义的新压力。
 该网络包括40多个新纳粹站点，提供商品，其中包括NaziSS符号和贴纸，以表扬凯尔·里滕豪斯（Kyle Rittenhouse）。这名少年因8月在威斯康星州枪杀两名黑死病抗议者而被调查。
  伦敦反数字仇恨中心（CCDH）表示，该产品的销售为乌克兰的两个新纳粹极端主义运动提供了资助：亚速（Azov）营和失灵部。据信，Azov营招募了许多英国极右翼激进分子参加乌克兰冲突。
 一名英国男子正面临审判，被控犯有十二项恐怖罪行，之后警方发现他拥有极端主义物质，包括与失教罪案有关的衣服和旗帜。学生否认这些指控。
 在Facebook对滥用行为进行治安审查时，它试图证明自己正在解决仇恨言论和极端主义。但是，该报告是由联盟更安全的网络共同撰写的，该报告说，这家社交媒体公司两年前首次被警告要使用新纳粹网络，自那以后，该网络有了很大的发展。
 CCDH首席执行官伊姆兰·艾哈迈德（Imran Ahmed）说：“ Facebook的领导层让新纳粹分子通过Facebook和Instagram为他们的活动提供资金，从而危害了公共安全。
“两年前，Facebook被首次告知这个问题，但未能采取行动。”
 艾哈迈德（Ahmed）也是政府打击极端主义委员会试点小组的成员，他补充说：“极端分子不会注意到这种缺乏行动，现在他们知道Facebook允许他们在不干涉的情况下招募，组织和筹集资金。”
 周四，拥有Instagram的Facebook首次披露了该平台上仇恨言论盛行的数字，并显示在7月，8月和9月期间，大约每1000个观看的内容中就有仇恨言论。 Facebook还表示，在过去三个月中，已对2210万条仇恨言论内容采取了行动。
  亚速（Nov）营和失灵师似乎正试图将其意识形态向西方输出，据报道后者与“志趣相投”的团体之间存在联系，例如受禁的英国恐怖组织“国家行动”。反恐智囊团搜房中心（Soufan Center）估计，来自英国的10名“外国战斗人员”曾为乌克兰民兵服务，例如亚速夫营（Azov Battalion），后者以伊斯兰国等极端主义团体的方式使用了精美的宣传视频。据悉，新纳粹准军事部队亚速夫营还接待了美国极右翼组织“上升运动”的成员，该组织的成员因在夏洛茨维尔举行的2017年“团结右翼”集会中因暴力而入狱。
 它的Facebook页面之一被称为“煤气室”。访客被定向到可提供其他产品的站点，包括站在一个被谋杀的黑人和一个犹太人旁边的白色光头形象，并写着：“严重袭击：这可能发生在您身上。”
 CCDH的研究还发现，极端分子正在使用加密消息应用程序Telegram来组织他们在主流平台上的工作，处理付款和进行宣传。
 在与观察员联系后，Facebook开始删除新纳粹材料。后来，公司发言人说：“我们删除了违反我们禁止危险组织政策的内容。我们会定期改进技术，以更快地找到和删除此内容，并且，尽管还有更多工作要做，但我们正在取得进步。我们已经从Facebook和Instagram禁止了250多个白人至上主义组织。”
note_en:
Tech giant criticised for failing to act after being told two years ago about extremist activity on its platform
    A white supremacist network with more than 80,000 followers and links to the UK far right, including a student charged with terrorist offences, is being hosted by  Facebook and Instagram, putting the world’s largest social media company under fresh pressure to tackle extremism.
 The network, which includes more than 40 neo-Nazi sites, offers merchandise including NaziSS symbols and stickers praising  Kyle Rittenhouse, the teenager under investigation for shooting dead two Black Lives Matter protesters in Wisconsin in August.
  According to the London-based  Centre for Countering Digital Hate (CCDH), sales of the products fund two neo-Nazi extremist movements operating from Ukraine: Azov Battalion and Misanthropic Division. A number of UK far-right activists are believed to have been recruited by Azov Battalion  to fight in the conflict in Ukraine.
 A British man is facing trial  accused of 12 terror offences after police found him in possession of extremist material, including clothing and flags linked to Misanthropic Division. The student denies the charges.
 The disclosure comes as Facebook, under scrutiny over its policing of abuses, attempts to prove it is tackling hate speech and extremism. However, the report, co-authored by the Coalition for a Safer Web, report says the social media company was first warned of the neo-Nazi network two years ago, and it has grown significantly since then.
 Imran Ahmed, chief executive of CCDH, said: “Facebook’s leadership endangered public safety by letting neo-Nazis finance their activities through Facebook and Instagram.
 “Facebook was first told about this problem two years ago and failed to act.”
 Ahmed, who is also a member of the pilot group for the government’s commission on countering extremism, added: “This lack of action would not have gone unnoticed by extremists, who now know that Facebook allows them to recruit, organise and raise funds without interference.”
 On Thursday, Facebook, which owns Instagram, disclosed for the first time figures on the prevalence of hate speech on the platform, revealing that during July, August and September approximately one in 1,000 pieces of content viewed included hate speech. Facebook also said it had taken action on 22.1 million pieces of hate speech content during the same three months.
  Azov Battalion and Misanthropic Division appear to be trying to export their ideology to the west, with reports of links between the latter and “like-minded” groups, such as the proscribed UK terror organisation National Action. The  Soufan Center, an anti-terrorism thinktank, estimates that 10 “foreign fighters” from the UK have served with Ukrainian militia like Azov Battalion, which uses slick propaganda videos in the manner of extremist groups such as Islamic State. Azov Battalion, a neo-Nazi paramilitary force, is also known to have hosted members of the Rise Above Movement, a far-right US group whose members were jailed for violence at the 2017 “Unite the Right” rally in Charlottesville.
 One of its Facebook pages is called “Gas Chambers”. Visitors are directed to sites where other products are available, including imagery of white skinheads standing next to a murdered black man and a Jewish man, with the wording: “Aggravated assault: It could happen to you.”
 Research by the CCDH also found that extremists were using the encrypted messaging app Telegram to organise their efforts on mainstream platforms, process payments and spread propaganda.
 After being contacted by the  Observer, Facebook began taking down the neo-Nazi material. Later, a company spokesperson said: “We have removed the content which violates our policies prohibiting dangerous organisations. We regularly work to improve our technology to find and remove this content faster, and, while there is more work to do, we are making progress. We’ve banned over 250 white supremacist organisations from Facebook and Instagram.”
title: Facebook被谴责通过英国链接托管新纳粹网络
title_en: Facebook condemned for hosting neo-Nazi network with UK links

（CNN）在第108年，美国邮政总局的年度“圣诞老人行动”信函写作活动正在数字化并正在全国范围内扩展，以适应当前的大流行。
 该计划允许儿童和家庭向圣诞老人写信，然后从12月4日开始在USPSOperationSanta.com上进行在线处理和共享。寄信后，美国境内任何人都可以上网并收信，并帮助实现孩子或家庭的假期愿望。公司还可以帮助采用信函作为团队。
 USPS发言人金·弗鲁姆（Kim Frum）表示，虽然每个人和每个人都可以写一封信，但该计划已开始帮助有需要的家庭和孩子。
   Frum在电子邮件中告诉CNN：“该计划一直是为那些可能没有能力提供除日常基本需求以外的其他手段的家庭提供节日礼物。”
 弗鲁姆说，在过去的108年中，作为“圣诞老人行动”计划的一部分，USPS已收到数十万封信。仅去年一年，就向寄给圣诞老人的信和收养他们的信的人发送了11,000多个包裹。
  Frum说，USPS于2017年首次在纽约市启动了USPS圣诞老人行动的在线试点。它在2018年扩展到了七个城市在线，在2019年扩展到了17个城市。该计划的数字化成功为今年的扩展铺平了道路。
 全数字化的决定是在全国范围内冠状病毒病例持续激增之际作出的，使该国可以应对后果，包括经济影响。
“今年，可能会有更多的家庭受到财务和情感上的影响。无论是因为距离还是实际损失，如果没有亲人，都很难庆祝假期。那些有需要的人将意味着现在有这么多人的世界。”
 根据约翰·霍普金斯大学（Johns Hopkins University）的数据，截至周二晚上，美国有超过1,130万例冠状病毒病例，并且有248,000多例与冠状病毒相关的死亡。
 弗鲁姆说：“ 2020年所面临的挑战在许多方面影响着个人和家庭。” “ COVID-19导致失业，暂时失业，以及可悲的是失去家人和朋友。再加上自然灾害的破坏，很容易看出为什么USPS圣诞老人行动计划比以往任何时候都重要。”
   那些给圣诞老人写信的人可以把它寄到带有圣诞老人的头等邮票的信封里，该信箱的地址是：北极，北极，埃尔夫路123号，圣诞老人88888。以及ID验证流程。完整说明可在Operation Santa网站上找到。
 根据USPS的说法，信件作者和信件收养人的身份都保持匿名，以保护隐私并“保留圣诞老人的精神”。
note_en:
(CNN)In its 108th year, the US Postal Service's annual " Operation Santa" letter writing campaign is going digital -- and expanding nationwide -- to adapt to the ongoing pandemic.
 The program allows children and families to write letters to Santa, which will then be processed and shared online beginning on December 4 at  USPSOperationSanta.com. Once the letters are live, anyone in the US can go online and adopt a letter, and help make a child or family's holiday wishes come true. Companies also can help adopt letters as teams.
 While anyone and everyone can write a letter, the program was started to help families and kids in need, said Kim Frum, a spokeswoman for USPS.
   "The program has always been about providing holiday gifts for families who may not have the means to provide for anything more than basic everyday needs," Frum told CNN in email.
 Over the last 108 years, the USPS has received hundreds of thousands of letters as part of the "Operation Santa" program, Frum said. Last year alone, more than 11,000 packages were sent to people who wrote to Santa and had their letters adopted.
  USPS first launched an online pilot of USPS Operation Santa in 2017 in New York City, Frum said. It expanded to seven cities online in 2018, and 17 cities in 2019. The success of the digitization of the program helped pave the way for this year's expansion.
 The decision to go fully digital comes as coronavirus cases continue to surge nationwide, leaving the nation to grapple with the consequences, including the economic impact.
 "This year, there are likely more families impacted financially and emotionally. It will be hard to celebrate the holidays without loved ones, whether because of distance or actual loss. But being able to provide even the tiniest bit of normalcy or spark of happiness to those in need would mean the world to so many people right now."
 As of Tuesday evening, there are more than 11.3 million coronavirus cases in the US, according to  Johns Hopkins University data, and more than 248,000 coronavirus-related deaths.
 "2020 has seen its share of challenges affecting individuals and families in so many ways," Frum said. "COVID-19 resulted in job losses, temporary unemployment, and, sadly, the loss of family and friends. Couple that with devastation from natural disasters, and it's easy to see why USPS Operation Santa program is more important than ever."
   Those writing a letter to Santa can send it in an envelope with a first-class stamp addressed to Santa Claus, at his address: Santa Claus, 123 Elf Road, North Pole 88888. Those looking to adopt a letter must go through a short registration and ID verification process first.  Full instructions are available on the Operation Santa website.
 According to USPS, the identities of both the letter writers and the letter adopters are kept anonymous for privacy and to "preserve the spirit of Santa."
title: 美国邮政局宣布全国范围内的数字“圣诞老人行动”
title_en: US Postal Service Announces a Nationwide Digital 'Operation Santa'

note:
6月的一天，马克·扎克伯格（Mark Zuckerberg）宣布Facebook达到了一个新水平：每月有20亿活跃用户。该数字是该公司在衡量自身规模时首选的“度量”，意味着上个月有20亿不同的人在使用Facebook。很难理解那是多么的与众不同。请记住，facebook –最初的名称–于2004年专门为哈佛学生开设。没有人类企业，没有新技术，实用程序或服务被如此广泛地采用。吸收速度远远超过了互联网本身，更不用说电视，电影院或广播等古老技术了。
 同样令人惊奇的是：随着Facebook的发展，其用户对它的依赖也越来越多。人数的增加并没有像人们预期的那样伴随着较低的敬业度。更多并不意味着更糟–至少从Facebook的角度来看更糟。反之。在遥远的2012年10月，Facebook吸引了10亿用户，其中每天有55％的用户在使用它。达到20亿，占66％。它的用户群以每年18％的速度增长–对于您已经如此庞大的企业来说，您认为这是不可能的。 Facebook是已登录用户的最大竞争对手，其致命竞争对手Alphabet（前身为Google）拥有YouTube，位居第二，每月拥有15亿用户。接下来的四个最大的应用程序或服务中的三个，或任何想称其为服务的服务，分别是WhatsApp，Messenger和Instagram，分别拥有12亿，12亿和7亿用户（中国应用程序微信是另一个，拥有889个）百万）。这三个实体有一个共同点：它们都归Facebook所有。难怪该公司以4450亿美元的市值在全球排名第五。
 扎克伯格发布有关Facebook规模的新闻时附带了一条公告，该公告可能会或可能不会很重要。他说，该公司正在更改其“使命声明”，即美国公司所钟爱的虔诚版本。 Facebook的使命曾经是“使世界更加开放和互联”。非Facebook读者可能会问：为什么？连接本身就是目的，这是固有的，自动的。是吗？ Flaubert对火车持怀疑态度，因为他认为（在朱利安·巴恩斯（Julian Barnes）的表述中）“铁路只会让更多的人来回走动，相遇和变得愚蠢。”您不必像Flaubert那样厌世，就可以想出类似的东西是否存在在Facebook上联系人们并不是真的。例如，人们普遍认为，Facebook在唐纳德·特朗普的选举中发挥了重要甚至是至关重要的作用。对人类的好处尚不清楚。扎克伯格似乎已经想到了这种想法，或者类似的想法，因为新的使命宣言阐明了所有这种联系的原因。它说，新的使命是“赋予人们建立社区并使世界更紧密团结的力量”。
 嗯Alphabet的使命宣言是“组织世界的信息，使其普遍可访问和有用”，伴随着格言“不要作恶”，这已成为许多嘲笑的根源：史蒂夫·乔布斯称其为“废话”。 。 1是，但不仅仅是胡说八道。许多公司，甚至整个行业，都将其商业模式立足于邪恶。例如，保险业务取决于保险人向客户收取的费用超过其保险价值的事实。这很公平，因为如果他们不这样做，他们将无法像企业一样生存。不公平的是，许多保险公司都使用愤世嫉俗的技术来尽可能避免在发生针对被保险人的事件时付款。只需问一个拥有财产的人遭受重大不幸。值得一提的是“别作恶”，因为很多企业都是。在互联网世界中，这尤其是一个问题。互联网公司正在一个客户和监管者不了解（如果根本不了解）的领域中工作。他们所做的事情，如果有什么好处，就定义而言是新的。在这个新奇，无知和不受监管的重叠领域中，非常值得提醒员工不要邪恶，因为如果公司成功并成长，那么随之而来的是很多邪恶的机会。
 Google和Facebook都从一开始就走这条路。他们这样做的风格是不同的。我认识的一位互联网企业家曾与两家公司打过交道。他告诉我：“ YouTube知道他们正在进行很多肮脏的事情，并热衷于尝试并做些有益的事情来缓解这种情况。”我问他“肮脏”是什么意思。 ‘恐怖和极端主义的内容，被盗的内容，侵犯版权的行为。那种事但是以我的经验，Google知道他们所做的某些事情存在模棱两可，道德上的疑问，至少他们会考虑一下。 Facebook不在乎。当您和他们一起在一个房间里时，您可以分辨出来。他们是–他花了点时间找到正确的单词–“模糊”。
 这听起来很刺耳。但是，自Facebook创立以来，一直存在伦理问题和模棱两可的事实，我们知道这一事实，因为Facebook的创建者当时在实时博客中。就像阿伦·索金（Aaron Sorkin）拍摄的有关Facebook的诞生的电影《社交网络》中叙述的那样。在哈佛的第一年，扎克伯格遭受了浪漫的拒绝。谁不会通过创建一个将大学生的图片并排放置的网站来对此做出回应，以便该网站的用户可以投票选出他们认为更有吸引力的图片？ （这部电影看起来好像只有女大学生：在现实生活中两者都是。）该网站名为Facemash。用伟人自己的话来说：
 我有点陶醉，我不会说谎。那如果不是晚上10点呢现在是星期二晚上？什么？ Kirkland宿舍Facebook在我的桌面上打开，其中一些人有非常可怕的Facebook图片。我几乎想将其中一些面孔放在一些农场动物的图片旁边，并让人们投票，哪个更具吸引力……让黑客开始吧。
正如蒂姆·吴（Tim Wu）在其充满活力的原创新书《注意力商人》（The Attention Merchants）中所解释的那样，扎克伯格在此使用的“ facebook”在传统上是指美国大学制作的一本物理小册子，以“嗨，我的名字是”在活动上贴；这些页面是由排成一排的带有相应名称的头像组成的。哈佛大学已经在研究其各种宿舍Facebook的电子版本。领先的社交网络Friendster已经拥有300万用户。将这两件事放在一起的想法并不完全是新颖的，但是正如扎克伯格当时所说的那样，‘我认为大学要花几年的时间才能做到这一点有点可笑。我可以比他们做得更好，而且我可以在一周内做到。’
 Wu认为，吸引和转售注意力一直是许多现代企业的基本模式，从19世纪后期的巴黎的海报到发明大众市场报纸（这些报纸不是通过流通而是通过广告销售来赚钱）到广告和电视广告的现代产业。尽管这类公司可能是有史以来最纯粹的例子，但其业务是吸引和出售注意力，而Facebook却是这类公司的一员。它的创造涉及很少的新思想。正如Wu所言，Facebook是“发明与成功比率极低的企业”。扎克伯格所拥有的而不是独创性是能够完成工作并清楚地看到重大问题的能力。互联网创业公司的关键是执行计划和适应不断变化的环境的能力。扎克（Zuck）的技能-聘请才华横溢的工程师，并驾驭行业的大趋势-使他的公司发展到今天。 Facebook巨人旗下的两家巨大的姊妹公司Instagram和WhatsApp分别以10亿美元和190亿美元的价格被收购，当时他们没有任何收入。没有银行家，分析师或贤哲能告诉扎克伯格这些收购的价值。没有人比他更了解。他可以看到事情的发展趋势，并帮助他们实现目标。那个才华横溢了数千亿美元。
 正如前Facebook经理安东尼奥·加西亚·马丁内斯（AntonioGarcíaMartínez）在《混沌猴子》（Chaos Monkeys）中论证的那样，杰西·艾森伯格（Jesse Eisenberg）在扎克伯格（Theuckerberg）上出色的肖像令人误解，这是关于他在公司任职的有趣的刻薄书。电影扎克伯格是一个高度可信的人物，是计算机天才，位于自闭症谱系中的某个地方，社交技能很少或根本不存在。但这不是那个男人真正的样子。在现实生活中，扎克伯格正在攻读计算机科学双学位的学位，而心理学则是人们往往会忘记的部分。频谱上的人们对别人的思维方式的理解有限。据说自闭症患者缺乏“心理理论”。扎克伯格，不是很多。他非常了解人们的思维方式，尤其是知名度和地位的社会动态。 Facebook的最初发行仅限于拥有哈佛电子邮件地址的人；目的是使对该网站的访问看起来像是排他性的和理想的。 （并控制站点流量，以使服务器永不停机。心理学和计算机科学齐头并进。）然后，它扩展到了美国的其他精英园区。当它在英国推出时，仅限于Oxbridge和LSE。当时的想法是人们想看看自己喜欢的人在做什么，看看自己的社交网络，进行比较，夸耀和炫耀，充分控制每一刻渴望和嫉妒的时刻，以使自己的鼻子紧贴他人生活的甜品店橱窗。
 这一关注点吸引了Facebook的第一个外部投资者的关注，即如今臭名昭著的硅谷亿万富翁彼得·泰尔（Peter Thiel）。社交网络再次证明了这一点：2004年Thiel的50万美元投资对于公司的成功至关重要。但是，Facebook有一个特殊的原因吸引了Thiel的眼球，这源于知识历史的发展。泰尔（Thiel）在斯坦福大学（Stanford）攻读哲学专业的过程中，对美国法国哲学家RenéGirard的思想产生了兴趣，这是他最有影响力的著作《世界成立以来的隐藏事物》（Things Hidden）所倡导的。吉拉德（Girard）的一个大主意是他所说的“模仿欲望”。人类天生就需要食物和住所。一旦获得了这些基本的生活必需品，我们就会环顾四周，看看别人在做什么和想要什么，然后我们将其复制。在Thiel的摘要中，想法是“模仿是一切行为的根源”。
 吉拉德（Girard）是一位基督徒，他对人性的看法是人性的堕落。我们不知道我们想要什么或我们是谁；我们真的没有自己的价值观和信念；相反，我们拥有的是复制和比较的本能。我们是模仿者。 ‘人是不知道该渴望什么的人，他会转向别人以下定决心。我们渴望别人想要的东西，是因为我们效仿了他们的愿望。’环顾四周，小小比较。蒂尔（Thiel）如此狂热地进入Facebook的原因是，他第一次在Facebook中看到了以Girardian为核心的业务：建立在人们强烈的复制需求上。蒂尔说：“ Facebook首先是通过口耳相传，而且是关于口耳相传，因此它是双重模仿。” “事实证明，社交媒体比它看起来要重要，因为它关乎我们的本性。”我们渴望被人们看到，我们渴望看到它，而Facebook是人类有史以来最流行的工具。
 这些想法暗示的人性T太暗了。如果所有人都想去看看别人，以便他们可以与自己比较并复制自己想要的东西-如果那是关于人类及其动机的最终，最深刻的真相-那么Facebook真的不需要人类的福利遭受了太多麻烦，因为发生在我们身上的所有坏事都是我们对自己所做的事情。对于公司在使命宣言中提出的所有建议，Facebook是一家以假性为前提的公司。也许正是出于这个原因，Facebook比其他任何一家规模的公司都充满了恶性循环。引人注目的小报版本以事件的形式出现，例如强奸，自杀，谋杀和杀害警察的现场直播。但是，在我看来，这是Facebook相对无罪的领域之一。人们在网站上直播这些可怕的事情，因为它吸引了最多的受众。如果Snapchat或Periscope更大，他们会在那做。
 然而，在许多其他地区，该站点并非无可非议。该公司的最引人注目的最近的批评来自其在特朗普的选举角色干。这样做有两个组成部分，其中之一隐含在站点的性质中，这具有将用户细分和细分成志同道合的群体的内在趋势。在实际中，“联系”的任务意味着要与同意您的人联系。我们无法证明这些“滤泡”对我们的社会有多危险，但显然，它们对我们日趋分散的政体产生了严重影响。我们对“我们”的概念越来越狭窄。
这种分裂为去年英美政治灾难中Facebook的第二种罪恶创造了条件。 Portmanteau对这些事态发展的称呼是“假新闻”和“后真相”，这是由于从一般公众辩论的退缩变成了独立的意识形态掩体而得以实现的。在露天，可以对假新闻进行辩论和揭露。在Facebook上，如果您不是被骗人的社区成员，那么您很可能永远不会知道他们正在流传。至关重要的是，Facebook在讲真话方面没有经济利益。没有公司能更好地体现互联网时代的教义，即如果产品免费，那么您就是产品。 Facebook的客户不是网站上的用户：Facebook的客户是使用其网络并喜欢将广告定向到受众的能力的广告商。 Facebook为什么要关心网站上流传的新闻是否是假的？它的兴趣在于定位，而不是内容。这可能是公司使命声明发生变化的原因之一。如果您唯一的兴趣是与人联系，那么您为什么要担心虚假？它们甚至可能比事实要好，因为它们可以更快地识别志趣相投的人。 “建立社区”这一新发现的野心使公司似乎对它所建立的联系产生了更多的兴趣。
 正如Facebook所承认的那样，假新闻并不是影响2016年总统大选结果的唯一方法。 2017年1月6日，国家情报总监发布了一份报告，称俄罗斯人发动了一场互联网虚假宣传运动，以损害希拉里·克林顿并帮助特朗普。 “莫斯科的影响力运动遵循了俄罗斯的信息战略，该战略将秘密情报行动（例如网络活动）与俄罗斯政府机构，国家资助的媒体，第三方中介以及付费社交媒体用户或“巨魔”的公开努力相结合。”报告说。 4月底，Facebook在其内部安全部门发表的一篇有趣的论文中承认了这个（当时）相当明显的事实。他们认为，“虚假新闻”是一个无用的，笼统的术语，因为错误信息实际上以多种方式传播：
 信息（或影响）运作–政府或有组织的非国家行为者为扭曲国内或国外政治气氛而采取的行动。
 虚假新闻–伪装成事实的新闻文章，但其中包含有故意的事实陈述，目的是引起人们的热情，吸引观众或欺骗他人。
 虚假放大器–通过虚假陈述进行的协调活动，目的是操纵政治讨论（例如，阻止特定政党参与讨论，或在其他人身上散布耸人听闻的声音）。
 虚假信息–故意传播的不正确或操纵的信息/内容。这可能包括虚假新闻，也可能涉及更微妙的方法，例如虚假标记操作，向无辜的中间人提供不正确的报价或故事，或者故意放大有偏见或误导性的信息。
 该公司承诺将认真对待这个问题或一系列问题，就像对待其他问题一样，例如恶意软件，帐户黑客和垃圾邮件。我们拭目以待。一个人的假新闻是另一个人的真实话语，而Facebook努力避免对其网站上的内容负责-除了色情内容外，该内容非常严格。纳瑞（Nary）上露乳头。这是一组奇怪的优先事项，仅在美国背景下才有意义，在这种情况下，任何明显的性欲都会立即使该网站因不道德行为而享有声誉。禁止哺乳妇女的照片，并迅速将其取下来。说谎和宣传都可以。
理解这一点的关键是考虑广告客户的需求：他们不想出现在乳房图片旁边，因为这可能会损害他们的品牌，但是他们不介意与谎言并列出现，因为谎言可能会帮助他们找到乳房。他们想要定位的消费者。乔纳森·塔普林（Jonathan Taplin）在对“数字时代的强盗男爵”的辩论中指出，“在快速移动和破坏事物”中，他对Buzzfeed进行了分析：与主要新闻媒体（例如《纽约时报》，《华盛顿邮报》，《赫芬顿邮报》，NBC新闻等）的头条新闻相比，这引起了更多的参与。
 事实是，欺诈性内容和被盗内容在Facebook上盛行，该公司并不介意，因为这并不符合其利益。网站上的许多视频内容都是从创建它的人那里窃取的。拍摄高品质的解释性短片的德国公司Kurzgesagt的一幅有启发性的YouTube视频，指出在2015年，Facebook观看次数最高的一千个视频中有725个被盗。这是Facebook利益与社会利益冲突的另一个领域。我们可能集体有兴趣维持许多不同形式和平台上的创造性和富有想象力的工作。 Facebook没有。正如Martínez在《混沌猴子》中解释的那样，它具有两个优先事项：增长和货币化。它根本不在乎内容的来源。只是现在才开始关心大部分内容是欺诈性的感觉，因为如果这种感觉变得普遍，那可能会影响信任度，从而影响人们对网站的时间。
 扎克伯格本人在Facebook帖子中谈到了“ Facebook和选举”问题，对此发表了讲话。经过一定程度的胡说八道（“我们的目标是使每个人都有发言权。我们深信于人”），他对此轻描淡写。 ‘在Facebook上的所有内容中，人们看到的内容中有99％以上是真实的。虚假新闻和恶作剧的数量很少。’超过一个Facebook用户指出，扎克伯格在自己的新闻提要中发表的关于真实性的帖子仅次于虚假新闻。在一个案例中，假故事是来自电视体育频道ESPN。什么时候
